question:
Problem B-1
Evaluate the infinite product
\[
\prod_{n=2}^{\infty} \frac{n^{3}-1}{n^{3}+1}
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Problem B-1
Evaluate the infinite product
\[
\prod_{indexer=2}^{\infty} \frac{indexer^{3}-1}{indexer^{3}+1}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem B-1
Evaluate the infinite product
\[
\prod_{marigolds=2}^{\infty} \frac{marigolds^{3}-1}{marigolds^{3}+1}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem B-1
Evaluate the infinite product
\[
\prod_{fixedconstant=2}^{\infty} \frac{fixedconstant^{3}-1}{fixedconstant^{3}+1}
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Evaluate the infinite product
\[
\prod_{n=3}^{\infty} \frac{n^{3}-8}{n^{3}+8}.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Write the partial products
\[
P_k:=\prod_{n=3}^{k}\frac{n^{3}-8}{n^{3}+8},\qquad P=\lim_{k\to\infty}P_k.
\]

1. Factor each cubic:
\[
 n^{3}-8=(n-2)(n^{2}+2n+4),\qquad n^{3}+8=(n+2)(n^{2}-2n+4).
\]
Hence
\[
P_k=\Bigl(\prod_{n=3}^{k}\frac{n-2}{n+2}\Bigr)\Bigl(\prod_{n=3}^{k}\frac{n^{2}+2n+4}{n^{2}-2n+4}\Bigr).
\]

2. Linear part:
\[
\prod_{n=3}^{k}\frac{n-2}{n+2}=\frac{(k-2)!}{5\cdot6\cdots(k+2)}=\frac{24}{k(k-1)(k+1)(k+2)}.
\]

3. Quadratic part. Put $A_n=n^{2}+2n+4$. Then $n^{2}-2n+4=A_{n-2}$, so
\[
\prod_{n=3}^{k}\frac{n^{2}+2n+4}{n^{2}-2n+4}=\frac{A_{k-1}A_k}{A_1A_2}.
\]
Because $A_1=7$, $A_2=12$, $A_{k-1}=k^{2}+3$, and $A_k=k^{2}+2k+4$, this equals
\[
\frac{(k^{2}+3)(k^{2}+2k+4)}{84}.
\]

4. Combine:
\[
P_k=\frac{24}{k(k-1)(k+1)(k+2)}\cdot\frac{(k^{2}+3)(k^{2}+2k+4)}{84}=\frac{2}{7}\cdot\frac{(k^{2}+3)(k^{2}+2k+4)}{k(k-1)(k+1)(k+2)}.
\]
The rational factor tends to $1$ because the numerator and denominator are quartic polynomials with the same leading coefficient. Therefore
\[
P=\lim_{k\to\infty}P_k=\frac{2}{7}.
\]

Thus
\[
\boxed{\displaystyle\prod_{n=3}^{\infty}\frac{n^{3}-8}{n^{3}+8}=\frac{2}{7}.}
\]

core_steps: Truncate the infinite product at k and view the limit as k→∞
Write the finite product as a ratio of linear and quadratic factors
Observe that matching factors from successive n cancel (telescoping)
After cancellations, simplify the leftover expression and take the limit

slot1.description: Starting index of the product; any integer ≥2 keeps the same factorisation-and-telescoping proof

slot2.description: The fixed cube 1 in n³±1 may be replaced by any positive integer a (using (n³−a³)/(n³+a³) and beginning the product at n=a+1); the same factorisation and telescoping steps apply
